metadata.title: Supplemental Material for Big Data, Actually: Examining Systematic Messaging in 188 Romantic Comedies Using Unsupervised Machine Learning

abstract:
Popular films within the romantic comedy genre set social expectations for romantic relationships, sexual activity, and gender roles, especially for young audiences. Nevertheless, academic research on the genre is scarce and mostly limited to manual content analyses conducted on relatively small samples of films. To systematically examine trends in thematic content and social messages in romantic comedy scripts over time, we harness a novel computational method, the ANalysis of Topic Model Network to analyze 188 scripts of top-grossing romantic comedies in the United States between 1980 and 2019. We estimate the dynamic prevalence of themes in the dialogue and demonstrate the increasing focus on romantic relationships at the expense of other life aspects in recent decades. We demonstrate how this trend systematically resulted in an increase in the prominence of misleading and detrimental messages about romantic relationships. The theoretical, social, and practical implications of our findings are discussed.

This study uses a computational method for the analysis of big textual data to examine the scripts of almost 200 popular romantic comedies. An analysis over time of changes in discourse between 1980 and 2019 reveal an increasing focus on romantic relationships at the expense of other life aspects, such as work and religion. We complemented the bird's-eye view computational analysis with close reading and analysis of the specific topics that were prevalent in the films over time.

paragraphs: Given their predictable plotlines, superficial characters, and simplistic happy endings, romantic comedies (romcoms) are often considered unworthy of in-depth scrutiny from researchers who have examined the genre (Johnson & Holmes, 2009;McDonald, 2007;Mortimer, 2010) and media critics 1 . Although these critiques are not unique to romcoms (e.g., discussions of Star Trek by Jenkins, 1988), they are consistently present in the discussion of the romcom genre specifically (McDonald, 2007;Mortimer, 2010). Nevertheless, romcoms are immensely popular among audiences, with 76% of adults in the United States reporting a favorable impression of the genre (Morning Consult & The Hollywood Reporter, 2018). Romcoms are also economically successful (Nash, 2010); with the ten top-grossing romcoms alone earning a combined $1.7 billion dollars (BoxOffice Mojo, n.d.). Beyond serving a function of entertainment, romcoms reflect and shape cultural meanings around romantic ideas, such as "love at first sight" and "love conquers all." Young adults are particularly receptive to these depictions and may seek information about romantic relationships from the media. Evidentially, young people often cite romcoms as inspiration when describing ideal romantic relationships (Bachen & Illouz, 1996;Hefner & Wilson, 2013). Romcoms also influence romantic beliefs (Sprecher & Metts, 1989) about idealized relationships (Driesmans et al., 2016); traditional gender roles, passionate love, sexual interactions, selfesteem (Erickson & Dal Cin, 2018), and even obsessive stalking behavior (Lippman, 2018).

Beyond the potential effects of observing characters modeling behaviors (Bandura, 1986); the genre's reliance on recycled plot lines, character types, and messages (Mortimer, 2010) may provide the consistent and repetitive messaging necessary for cultivation effects, especially for heavy viewers (Gerbner, 1998). Still, little systematic study has explored the content of romcoms over time. Instead, scholars analyzing the genre often use qualitative approaches, with relatively small samples of films to identify recurring categories and messages (Deleyto, 2003;Hefner & Wilson, 2013;McDonald, 2007). Although this work contributed to understanding the genre, broader systematic study is needed, with additional attention to changes over time.

Accordingly, we utilize a novel data-driven computational method to analyze 188 scripts from top-grossing romcoms in American markets (documented by BoxOfficeMojo.com). Using the multistep unsupervised ANalysis of Topic Model Networks Method, ANTMN (Walter & Ophir, 2019), we systematically dissect the linguistic and thematic content of romcoms over time, identifying prevalent topics and broad themes to evaluate changes between 1980 and 2019. Finally, we examine the prevalence of misleading social messages about romantic relationships in each theme and analyze trends in misleading messages over time.

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
sections.header_raw: The Romantic Comedy Genre and Its Social Messages
sections.header_norm: the romantic comedy genre and its social messages
paragraphs: Defining romantic comedy can prove difficult, as genre itself is a concept that many scholars find debatable (see Deleyto, 2003). Further, romantic themes are pervasive across many genres, including westerns and action films. McDonald (2007;p. 9) defined romantic comedy broadly as "a film which has as its central narrative motor a quest for love, which portrays this quest in a light-hearted way and almost always to a successful conclusion." Deleyto (2003;p. 45) asserts a romantic comedy must include a narrative with "historically and culturally specific views of love, desire, sexuality and gender relationships," while offering a transformative space and humorous perspective on the characters' relationships and development. Additional definitions exist for subgenres within romcoms, such as the screwball and the neo-traditional (McDonald, 2007).

Rather than inefficiently define the boundaries of our corpus following these often-contradictory definitions, we referenced a list of top-grossing films (in American markets), as other researchers have done (Hefner & Wilson, 2013). Specifically, we utilized a list of top-grossing romcoms from BoxOffice-Mojo.com (BOM; Box Office Mojo, n.d.), a reliable, commonly used source in film research (Johnson & Holmes, 2009;Lippman, Ward, & Seabrook, 2014) and one the public also utilizes. Simply put, we define romcoms as films presented to the public as romcoms.

The focus on the protagonists' relationship (almost uniformly in the form of heterosexual courtship) has remained relatively stable throughout the evolution of the romance genre in general, and the romcom specifically. The social context for romance, however, evolves over time, conforming to and at times challenging social issues and dilemmas, such as the need to satisfy both partners (rather than men only) and the difficulties of balancing work and romance (Swidler, 1980). The tone and perspectives in discussing issues such as romantic obsession (Alberti, 2013) and marriage Note. To the right are the top 10 words most associated with each topic. Notice that it would be hard to decipher the meaning of a topic from these top words alone. Thus, labels were chosen based on close readings of full scenes representing each topic.

have accommodated audiences' ever-changing needs and beliefs (Johnson & Holmes, 2009). These changing portrayals of romantic standards and ideals in cultural objects may at the same time reflect and shape social norms and perceptions about relationships (Hefner & Wilson, 2013). Films serve as agents for social learning, the process by which audiences form beliefs about real world behaviors and their consequences by observing others (Bandura, 1986). Romcoms have been associated with such social effects, both in research and anecdotally (Bachen & Illouz, 1996;Galician, 2004).

These viewing effects could be cumulative, particularly in the case of cohesive and consistent genres, in the sense that heavy exposure to similar messages across films could lead to genre-consistent beliefs (Gerbner et al., 1986;Gerbner, 1998). Indeed, qualitative content analyses point to several consistent thematic patterns and trends in romcoms, focusing on the genre's definition of romantic ideals and how those depictions are sometimes challenged. Johnson and Holmes (2009) analyzed 40 popular romcoms from 1995 to 2005 and found the most common themes related to affection, commitment, and demonstrating care. Examining differences in depiction of gender roles, they found male characters initiate most relationships, and are more likely to provide compliments, gifts, and favors to female characters.

Other messages highlighted the contradictory and confusing messages of the genre; for instance, depictions of couples arguing often result in their relationship ending, but heated arguments and conflict may also indicate passionate love. Similarly, although marriage is the end-goal of many romcoms, the couples' lives after marriage are often omitted or negatively portrayed (Johnson & Holmes, 2009). Overall, Johnson and Holmes (2009) concluded that relationships in romcoms present contradictory messages that could mislead audiences, who may hope to learn from observing the fates of their characters (Bandura, 1986;Galician, 2004).

In the broadest and most systematic analysis to date, Hefner and Wilson (2013) coded 52 top-grossing romcoms between 1998 and 2008 and identified four romantic ideals: the concept of a soulmate or one-and-only love, idealization of the other, love conquers all, and love at first sight. At least one romantic ideal was expressed in 98% of films, with one ideal expressed every 14 minutes on average. In terms of social learning (Bandura, 1986), ideal expressions were most often rewarded, while challenges to those ideals were typically punished. The soulmate ideal was the most common, comprising 40% of expressions in all films. Love conquers all was the most common overarching message for a film to employ, with 32 of the 39 films including an idealized theme relating to this idea. The Topic Community Note. Topics are colored by theme (Relationships in gray, Life in black), with the size of each label indicating the relative prominence of each in the corpus. The distance between topics represents a two-dimensional estimation of similarity, such that topics closer to each other are estimated to be co-occur more often in scenes. The network is weighted, fully connected and undirected, though some links may be too thin to be apparent.

Many of these idealized depictions are so prevalent in romcoms that they are identified as tropes, recycled portrayals of characters and their interactions. For instance, idealization as identified by Hefner and Wilson (2013) involves descriptions of a partner or romantic interest as exaggeratedly perfect, and may apply to the partner's attractiveness specifically or to sexual compatibility (Galician, 2004) as well as other relationship components such as an expectation of mind-reading (Segrin & Nabi, 2002) or eternal love (Allen, 1999). Other tropes include bickering and acerbic banter as an indication of compatibility (Galician, 2004), whereas the "battle of the sexes" can insinuate or explicitly argue that men and women have goals or traits that are inherently or even biologically incompatible (McDonald, 2007). Romcoms also rely on tropes such as "follow the heart" (choosing partners based on love rather than more rational indications of compatibility; Sprecher & Metts, 1989), love at first sight (including the fortuitous "meet cute"; McDonald, 2007;Mortimer, 2010), love is transformational (the idea that love can change a person for the better; Galician, 2004), and relationship priority (putting a romantic relationship above all else; Johnson & Holmes, 2009). There are also rescue fantasies, related to "damsels in distress" being rescued by strong men, and the double-standard of virginity, seen either in the appeal of a virginal woman (Galician, 2004) or the undesirable male virgin (Alberti, 2013). Although these tropes can be found in other genres as well (Deleyto, 2003;McDonald, 2007), they are particularly prominent in romcoms.

The identification of tropes and misleading messages in romantic comedies was crucial for understanding the role of the genre in promoting specific messages and arguments about love and relationships. It was made possible due to reliance on close reading of prominent scenes from high-profile films. Those scholars often examined not only the dialogues but also metatextual features, such as the use of music, lighting, costumes, and acting. The ability to go into such detail came at the expense of breadth. In our study, we extend and complement prior research on romantic comedies by changing the resolution of analysis from micro to macroanalysis of the genre (Jockers, 2013). We do so by focusing only on the scripts of film dialogues of almost 200 films. In the next section we discuss the potential, and limitations, of such an approach, and how it could complement close readings of individual texts.

In the next section we discuss the potential, and limitations, of such an approach, and how it could complement close readings of individual texts.
sections.preview_head: Defining romantic comedy can prove difficult, as genre itself is a concept that many scholars find debatable (see Deleyto, 2003). Further, romantic themes are pervasive across many genres, including westerns and action films. McDonald (2007;p. 9) defined romantic comedy broadly as "a film which has as its central narrative motor a quest for love, which portrays this quest in a light-hearted way and almost always to a successful conclusion." Deleyto (2003;p. 45) asserts a romantic comedy must include a narrative with "historically and culturally specific views of love, desire, sexuality and gender relationships," while offering a transformative space and humorous perspective on the characters' relationships and development. Additional definitions exist for subgenres within romcoms, such as the screwball and the neo-traditional (McDonald, 2007).

paragraphs: ). Such analysis will require immense resources (Krippendorff, 2019); and will also not be able to discern linguistic patterns that can only be detected through complex analyses of cooccurrences across large corpora (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013). Importantly, microanalysis does not reject the importance and utility of close reading. As argued by Jockers (2013), "the strength of the approach is that it allows for both zooming in and zooming out" (p. 23). Congruently, the method employed in this study (Walter & Ophir, 2019) is a mixed-method approach combining inductive computational identification of linguistic patterns and deductive, theory-driven analysis of texts annotated by the model.

The application of microanalysis of films' texts, while not capturing all aspects of the viewer's experience, has been shown to allow the examination of ideologies, messages, and language of films. For example, Murtagh et al. (2009) illustrate this by analyzing the text of Casablanca and several episodes of CSI: Las Vegas. Ultimately, although their analyses include no visual information, the reliance on text allowed them to conduct sophisticated hierarchical clustering and correspondence analysis and reveal important emotional changes in key scenes in Casablanca and clearly identified scenes leading into commercial breaks in CSI based on differences in language use, among other interesting findings. The authors therefore conclude that textual analysis of film and TV scripts provide a framework of important information regarding style and structure.

In another study, Fürsich (2009) harnessed textual analysis to illustrate systematic shifts that could not be identified via the close reading of individual documents. Their analysis opted to explain prevailing cultural ideologies, looking beyond individual audience interpretations of messages. Such textual analysis revealed, for example, which cultural depictions are more prominent across texts and over time, and which are often absent and omitted. Encouraging a combination of microanalysis and close reading, Fürsich notes that this type of analysis "supports media literacy efforts by helping audiences see through the compliance of media institutions and journalists" (2009; p. 48). We aim to apply the same logic to the analysis of romantic comedies, building upon prior close reading done via manual content analysis (Hefner & Wilson, 2013;Johnson & Holmes, 2009).

The validity of textual analysis of film's scripts received support from research utilizing textual representation of films (synopses) to predict which scripts are worth producing (a process known as "green-lighting", Eliashberg et al., 2007) or to create reliable summaries of films by extracting narrative information (Tsoneva et al., 2007). Similar to our approach here, those studies combined automated big-data methods with expert-based human coding. Despite limitations, such as the reliance on bag-of-words (i.e., not considering syntax and order) and the absence of metatextual features, Eliashberg et al. (2007) produce a model that successfully predicts return on investment based on the synopses alone. We expect our model, utilizing the full scripts of films, to offer even more detailed insight into the genre.

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
paragraphs: Previous examinations of romcoms described above, while important to our understanding of their thematic structure and social messages, have been limited by the number of texts considered, the methodological constraints of available tools for text analysis, and epistemological decisions. Relying on manual coding, these works analyze relatively few films at a time, usually sampling films based on their perceived cultural importance and relevance to specific subgenres (Deleyto, 2003;McDonald, 2007). The largest investigation of the genre, conducted by Hefner and Wilson (2013), included a corpus of 52 films spanning 10 years, but even this is a relatively short span and small sample compared to what is possible with computational methods.

Further, some of the previous deductive approaches have utilized predefined codebooks that may problematically assume that all categories had been theorized and identified beforehand, and that linguistic patterns found in prior work could be fully applied to other texts, created at different time, location and context (van Gorp, 2010). In addition, deductive approaches are often biased by the background, knowledge, and expectations of researchers (Walter & Ophir, 2019). By focusing on predefined romantic ideals, other messages or recurring themes might be missed if absent from the coding tool. Similarly, analyses relying on open coding, or film theorists developing a progression of the genre based on close readings of a few films per decade may lose sight of smaller trends or overarching themes, because humans simply are not as efficient at recognizing linguistic patterns as machines are (Walter & Ophir, 2019). Such approaches are integral to our understanding of media content and should not be taken for granted. However, they do not allow for the exploration that computational methodologies can provide.

Therefore, we opt for an inductive, data-driven approach, identifying linguistic patterns in texts using computational algorithms through unsupervised machine learning, which does not rely on predefined categories (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013). Specifically, we use the ANTMN approach (Walter & Ophir, 2019) to inductively identify linguistic patterns in romcom scripts, based on the common linguistic assumption of co-occurrence, that is, that words tending to appear together in the same documents tend also to share thematic content (Blei et al., 2003). The ANTMN approach is unique in its ability to detect both broader themes and their linguistic building-blocks, called topics, in large corpora. ANTMN does so, in part, by using an inductive approach for the identification of linguistic patterns, and deductive approaches for the interpretation of the findings via a qualitative, theory-driven step in postmodeling to label these themes and topics. For that step, Walter and Ophir (2019) recommend the use of domain-relevant expertise and theoretical perspectives to the postmodeling stage. ANTMN therefore utilizes a mixed-method approach led by computational analyses which allow more efficient and detailed content analyses which are then supplemented by qualitative content experts. Our analysis was guided by the following research questions:
Research Question 1: What recurring topics are discussed in romantic comedies between 1980-2019?
Research Question 2: Could these topics be clustered into consistent broad themes?
Research Question 3: How have those topics and themes evolved between 1980 and 2019?

As explained, the cultivation potential of romcoms is supported by the consistent messaging seen in prominent tropes. The primary methodology among cultivation approaches outlined by Gerbner (1969) is institutional analysis, which begins with detecting these patterns before investigating their influence on audiences and identifying motivations behind these messaging systems. Among other critics, Potter (2014) points out that many scholars attempting to "test" cultivation effects are straying from Gerbner's original intent by focusing on a single genre rather than the media landscape. While acknowledging that the exploration of the romantic comedy genre is such a deviation, there is still insight to be gained from a larger and more systematic approach of romantic comedy than previous scholars have undertaken. By improving our understanding of how messages have developed over time, we are better able to attribute meaning to institutional decisions and practices behind this dissemination.

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
paragraphs: Data collection, preparation, and analysis each involved multiple steps. First, we collected the full scripts of the top 200 highestgrossing romcoms in the United States between 1980 and 2019; as classified by BOM (BoxOfficeMojo, n.d.). Second, we processed the scripts and prepared the text for analysis. Third, using ANTMN (Walter & Ophir, 2019); we calculated a topic model based on words' co-occurrence in scenes. Fourth, we used ANTMN to calculate a network of topics, where each node is a topic and the edges between nodes represent their co-occurrence in the same scripted scenes. Fifth, we identified broader themes, operationalized as clusters of topics that tended to appear together in the same scenes using a community detection algorithm (Fastgreedy; Clauset et al., 2004). Sixth, we qualitatively analyzed the content and thematic meaning of individual topics and themes, based on distribution lists of top words (the words most uniquely associated with each topic, i.e., not common in other topics) and top documents (the scenes identified by the model to best represent each topic or theme). Finally, we used multiple regression and analysis of variance to examine the effects of time on thematic content.

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
paragraphs: Textual Data BOM's collection of the top 200 highest-grossing romcoms within the U.S. market includes several films produced outside of the United States, including the Bridget Jones trilogy. A complete list of these films and associated metadata can be found in Appendix A in the online supplemental materials. Scripts were obtained from free repositories, including Scripts.com, SpringfieldSpringfield.co.uk, SimplyScripts .com, and DailyScript.com. We were unable to find scripts for some relatively less popular films from the mid-1980s and early 1990s, specifically Best Friends, Micki & Maude, Murphy's Romance, Cousins, and Only the Lonely. Two additional films, Love at First Bite and Manhattan, were omitted as they fell outside of the time range of interest . We acknowledge that this cut-off may limit our ability to generalize to the full spectrum of movies in the romantic comedy genre, as it dates back several more decades. However, we have focused on the top-grossing films of all time, only two of which in the top 200 predate 1980. Thus, our analyses and findings reflect contemporary romcoms and do not constitute a historical analysis of the genre's roots.

Initial modeling revealed that a few films were distinct and disconnected from the rest of the corpus in their near-exclusive use of only two topics that grouped their language into the British vernacular of Victorian and Elizabethan eras. All were adaptations of classic novels by Jane Austen or the plays of William Shakespeare, released between 1993 and 2005. Importantly, these films rarely used other topics in the model and were therefore identified by their vernacular rather than their thematic content 2 . We therefore omitted these films: Much Ado About Nothing, Shakespeare in Love, Emma, Sense and Sensibility, and Pride and Prejudice 3 . Our final corpus thus consisted of 188 scripts.

Topic modeling requires researchers to define the level at which words appearing together are assumed to share thematic content, typically referred to as the "document level." Thus, as one might segment the themes and topics in a novel into individual chapters (Jockers, 2014); scripts were split into scenes. Scenes are considered the base unit of film, with a typical film including 40 to 60 scenes (Murtagh et al., 2009). In our corpus, the average was 63 scenes per film, ranging from 36 to 142. Many scripts included explicit indications for changes of scenes. In those that did not, the researchers made divisions manually, and watched the original film to identify and verify the separation of scenes when necessary. Scenes were divided by the authors based on changes in location, time, or characters, with two exceptions: instances of one conversation carried through multiple locations (a "walk and talk") and montages of quick cuts between time, place, and/or characters.

Some scripts were obtained as full screenplays complete with camera and stage directions (as from DailyScript.com), while others were transcripts meant for use as subtitles (as from Simply-Scripts.com), and still others fell somewhere in between. Given these differing formats, each was "cleaned" for comparability, including removal of stage directions and all other nondialogue (e. g., "(sighs)" or "CUT TO INT. RESTAURANT"). Although some of these directions may provide additional detail about the characters, authors and screenwriters were not consistent in their use of instructions, and they often included professional jargon that clouded our model (pertaining to editing and camera instructions and notes). More importantly, since many of our scripts did not include such directions at all, it was necessary to remove them so the scripts were equivalent and consistent. Next, we removed stopwords in multiple languages, from R's quanteda dictionaries (e.g., "the", "and") and additional stopwords recommended for novels and literature, including first and last names from Jockers (2014), punctuations, and numbers 4 . The final model consists of 188 films, broken into 11,900 scenes, with a vocabulary of 29,324 unique words (trimmed to 12,241 words after sparsity reduction 5 ; see Maier et al., 2018).

Textual Data BOM's collection of the top 200 highest-grossing romcoms within the U.S. market includes several films produced outside of the United States, including the Bridget Jones trilogy. A complete list of these films and associated metadata can be found in Appendix A in the online supplemental materials. Scripts were obtained from free repositories, including Scripts.com, SpringfieldSpringfield.co.uk, SimplyScripts .com, and DailyScript.com. We were unable to find scripts for some relatively less popular films from the mid-1980s and early 1990s, specifically Best Friends, Micki & Maude, Murphy's Romance, Cousins, and Only the Lonely. Two additional films, Love at First Bite and Manhattan, were omitted as they fell outside of the time range of interest . We acknowledge that this cut-off may limit our ability to generalize to the full spectrum of movies in the romantic comedy genre, as it dates back several more decades. However, we have focused on the top-grossing films of all time, only two of which in the top 200 predate 1980. Thus, our analyses and findings reflect contemporary romcoms and do not constitute a historical analysis of the genre's roots.

paragraphs: The Analysis of Topic Model Networks ANTMN (Walter & Ophir, 2019) uses unsupervised machine learning, combining topic modeling and network analysis for the automated identification of thematic content in big textual data. ANTMN follows three basic steps. First, a topic model using Latent Dirichlet allocation and Gibbs sampling (see Blei et al., 2003) groups words into "topics" as statistical entities; frequency distributions of words based on how commonly they co-occur across documents (in this case, romcom scenes). Topic models use a Bayesian generative approach, in which the model attempts to "mimic" the writing process of a given corpus of documents (Blei et al., 2003). The algorithm extracts a set of topics from which the given corpus could have been created. In topic models, every word in the corpus has a probability of appearing in each of the topics, and every document is composed as a mixture of all topics (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013). Thus, each scene in our corpus is a mixture of every topic identified in the model, with some topics having stronger loadings than others, that is, comprising a higher percentage of the scene. Further, topic modeling utilizes a "bagof-words" approach, meaning that narrative, location, and syntax are disregarded (Blei et al., 2003). To choose the optimal values for our model, we used 10-fold cross-validation comparing the perplexity scores of multiple models (see Walter et al., 2020), ranging between 10 and 120 topics. Based on this analysis we chose a model of 40 topics, with hyperparameter a = .01.

To interpret the topics, we examined three types of information; the words with the highest loading over each topic, the words that are both prevalent and exclusive to each topic (see FREX words in Roberts et al., 2014), and the full documents (scenes) most representative of each topic. Two independent coders analyzed and labeled each topic based on a close reading of the word lists and the full scenes, looking for common content that defined each. Labels for each topic were decided manually and in agreement between the two coders based on these readings. As our main analysis was computed on the broad themes rather than individual topics, we did not run additional validation for topic labels. We did however validate the broader themes (see end of Results section).

In ANTMN's second step, we used the topic model's "theta matrix" to calculate a network based on the associations between 2 While we did not consider the possibility that historical movies would differ substantially in language a-priori, and thus did not omit these films from our original corpus, the separation of those films from the rest of the linguistic network in the early iteration of the model illustrates ANTMN's sensitivity to, and ability to identify, unique language use.

3 A version of the model was presented at the International Communication Association Conference (Moore & Ophir, 2020). The broad themes emerging from both models were virtually the same. 4 The full list of stopwords is available in the Quanteda package in R (see https://quanteda.io/reference/stopwords.html) and was supplemented with the words suggested by Jockers, 2013 (https://www.matthewjockers .net/macroanalysisbook/expanded-stopwords-list/). A list of custom words added by the researchers can be found at https://osf.io/qgpmt/. 5 Sparsity reduction removes words that are so prevalent or so unique as to prevent the model from learning about the corpus, either because the word exists in nearly all topics or only occurs in one film. Zipf's law (Manning & Schutze, 1999, pp. 23-29) states that roughly half of terms in any vocabulary are only used once and will therefore be removed. This process, also called "relative pruning" is detailed by Maier et al. (2018).

topics. Here, nodes are the topics and the edges between them represent a calculation, using cosine similarity, of how often topics co-occur in the same scenes (Walter & Ophir, 2019). For example, if a topic about wedding dresses often appears in the same scenes as one about meeting the in-laws, the two will have a strong tie in the network. Topics that rarely or never appear together will have a weak tie.

Ultimately, a fully connected, weighted, and undirected network was created. In the third step of ANTMN, the topic network was broken into coherent communities of topics. We opted to use the popular "Fastgreedy" (Clauset et al., 2004) community detection algorithm that was found optimal for small highly connected networks (Walter & Ophir, 2019).

The final step in this process involves qualitative coding to label the topics and themes. Following ANTMN's guidelines (Walter & Ophir, 2019) and adhering to the standard procedures in topic modeling (Maier et al., 2018) and semantic network analysis (Baden, 2018), the labeling process is conducted by experts in the specific field of interest. Therefore, the topics and themes were labeled by the researchers, who are experts in mass media, entertainment, and romantic comedies. Notably, such qualitative process does not follow quantitative estimation of intercoder reliability. Instead, labels were assigned after careful examination and close reading of the most representative scenes for each topic and theme, with refinement done through discussion between the two authors.

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
paragraphs: Our first research question asked what major topics exist in romcoms between 1980 and 2019. Our model identified 40 topics, clustered two coherent and distinct clusters (themes). Figure 1 shows the topics organized in descending frequency (indicated by the length of the horizontal line) and colored by their association with either the first (black) or second (gray) themes. The bars are labeled with the topic names, and to the right of the bar are the ten words most strongly associated with each topic. Notably, it would be difficult to determine the subject of these topics based on these words lists alone. Thus, topic labels were determined based on close, qualitative readings of the most representative scenes.

Deeply analyzing each topic is beyond the scope of the current paper. Instead, we provide the labels and top words for each topic in Figure 1 and detail only the most prevalent topics: Breakups and Screwups (4.94%), Introductions (3.39%), Conflicting Feelings (3.56%), Mistakes and Separation (3.47%), and Friends or More (3.46%). Breakups and Screwups depicts interpersonal failures and despair, including relationships ending, unrequited love, and admitted mistakes. The most representative scene for this topic, that is, with the highest loading for the topic on the theta matrix at 75.23% (see Blei et al., 2003) comes from Someone Like You, depicting the breakup of Jane and Ray, ending with her crying as he walks away in the rain.

The second largest topic, Introductions, is defined by greetings and meetings. The most exemplary scene from this topic (79.74% topic loading) is from Crazy Rich Asians, when the protagonists arrive in Singapore; Nick reunites with his friends, Rachel meets those friends for the first time, and Rachel first sees this new country. The third most common topic, Conflicting Feelings, includes scenes of contradictory psychological experiences, such as simultaneous thanks and apologies, feeling seen but invisible, and being in a bad mood despite having fun. Often these scenes involve unbalanced partnerships, with one partner ready to commit while the other hesitates, as in an exemplary scene from Keeping the Faith (62.3% topic loading) where Anna suggests moving closer to Jake, who is not as enthusiastic about the idea as she had hoped. Other scenes employing this topic find characters discussing their troubles with sex, lack of relationship acceptance from their friends, or embarrassing themselves in front of their partners.

The next topic, Friends or More, deals with characters who are unsure if they like someone as more than a friend, which often involves them rejecting others' insinuations that the two are a romantic couple. For instance, the most exemplary scene comes from When Harry Met Sally . . . (77.74% topic loading). Here, Harry has convinced his friend Jess to try dating Sally. Uncertain, Jess asks why Harry doesn't date her if she's so great, but Harry insists that they are "just friends" despite describing her as attractive with a good personality. Finally, Mistakes and Separation includes characters discussing regrets or decisions that led them to separate from their partners or toward unhappiness more generally. The highest loading scene, at 79%, is the opening scene from Trainwreck, where the father of two little girls is explaining to them why he and their mother are divorcing. He explains this through a doll metaphor, describing how he does not want to play with the same doll for the rest of his life, and the scene ends with him coaxing the girls to repeat "monogamy isn't realistic."

The bars are labeled with the topic names, and to the right of the bar are the ten words most strongly associated with each topic. Notably, it would be difficult to determine the subject of these topics based on these words lists alone. Thus, topic labels were determined based on close, qualitative readings of the most representative scenes.

Deeply analyzing each topic is beyond the scope of the current paper. Instead, we provide the labels and top words for each topic in Figure 1 and detail only the most prevalent topics: Breakups and Screwups (4.94%), Introductions (3.39%), Conflicting Feelings (3.56%), Mistakes and Separation (3.47%), and Friends or More (3.46%). Breakups and Screwups depicts interpersonal failures and despair, including relationships ending, unrequited love, and admitted mistakes. The most representative scene for this topic, that is, with the highest loading for the topic on the theta matrix at 75.23% (see Blei et al., 2003) comes from Someone Like You, depicting the breakup of Jane and Ray, ending with her crying as he walks away in the rain.

The second largest topic, Introductions, is defined by greetings and meetings. The most exemplary scene from this topic (79.74% topic loading) is from Crazy Rich Asians, when the protagonists arrive in Singapore; Nick reunites with his friends, Rachel meets those friends for the first time, and Rachel first sees this new country. The third most common topic, Conflicting Feelings, includes scenes of contradictory psychological experiences, such as simultaneous thanks and apologies, feeling seen but invisible, and being in a bad mood despite having fun. Often these scenes involve unbalanced partnerships, with one partner ready to commit while the other hesitates, as in an exemplary scene from Keeping the Faith (62.3% topic loading) where Anna suggests moving closer to Jake, who is not as enthusiastic about the idea as she had hoped. Other scenes employing this topic find characters discussing their troubles with sex, lack of relationship acceptance from their friends, or embarrassing themselves in front of their partners.

The next topic, Friends or More, deals with characters who are unsure if they like someone as more than a friend, which often involves them rejecting others' insinuations that the two are a romantic couple. For instance, the most exemplary scene comes from When Harry Met Sally . . . (77.74% topic loading). Here, Harry has convinced his friend Jess to try dating Sally. Uncertain, Jess asks why Harry doesn't date her if she's so great, but Harry insists that they are "just friends" despite describing her as attractive with a good personality. Finally, Mistakes and Separation includes characters discussing regrets or decisions that led them to separate from their partners or toward unhappiness more generally. The highest loading scene, at 79%, is the opening scene from Trainwreck, where the father of two little girls is explaining to them why he and their mother are divorcing. He explains this through a doll metaphor, describing how he does not want to play with the same doll for the rest of his life, and the scene ends with him coaxing the girls to repeat "monogamy isn't realistic."

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
paragraphs: Our second research question asked what broad themes exist in popular romcoms (RQ2). The resulting network of topics from ANTMN is shown in Figure 2, Note that topics closer to each other tend to appear in the same scenes, however the network image is a two-dimensional projection, and thus an approximation of a multidimensional object (one dimension per topic). The first, gray community holds 63.8% of language on average per film (between 33.21% to 80.51%) and 63.77% on average per scene (between 3.29% to 99.12%). The black theme accounts for 36.2% of language on average per film (between 19.49% to 66.79%) and 36.23% on average per scene (between .88% to 96.71%).

To better understand the thematic meaning of each theme, we examined the individual topics comprising each and the most representative films and script scenes of each (i.e., scenes and full films with the highest relative use of this theme). Based on qualitative reading we labeled the gray theme Relationships and the black theme Life. The gray Relationships theme includes topics about the relationship between the two protagonists, that is, the couple at the heart of the central romance. This includes topics like Breakups and Screwups, Friends or More (examining the line between platonic and romantic relationships), Conflicting Feelings, and Past Marriages (i.e., marriages with partners who are not the protagonist couple). The films using the Relationships theme most extensively were Don Jon (80.5%), The Five-Year Engagement (76.39%), Just Go With It (75.96%), The Heartbreak Kid (75.9%) and How to Be Single (75.88%).

The black theme, dubbed Life, focused on aspects of life that are not directly about the couple, including their jobs, friends, mundane actions (e.g., shopping), or nonrelationship events (e.g., crimes or court hearings). The theme includes topics such as Phone Calls, Business Troubles, Financial Transactions, Travel and Weather. The films most extensively using the Life theme (hence focusing the least on relationships) were The American President (66.79%), Legal Eagles (61%), Pretty Woman (59.24%), Confessions of a Shopaholic (56.95%), and Down With Love (54.16%).

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
paragraphs: Harnessing the exceptional scope and breadth of our corpus, our third research question asked how the use of themes and topics has evolved over time (Research Question 3). Figure 3 demonstrates the change in percentage of language associated with the Relationships theme between 1980 and 2019 (Life being its inverse, as both accumulate to 100% in any scene). The regression line estimates a model without any covariates (y = À6.150318 þ .0034 years). The coefficient for years was highly significant (p , .001) and the model explained 12.85% (Adjusted R 2 ) of the variance in thematic content.

We next conducted an analysis of variance predicting thematic content over time. Consistent with the linear regression without covariates, age of the film increased the use of the Relationships theme (B = À.003, p , .001).

Next, we examined whether the use of individual topics changed over time. To avoid increasing the possibility of finding associations by chance alone due to multiple statistical comparisons, we examined only topics that were found in our qualitative reading to be theoretically related to the cultivation of romantic relationships and social expectations (Gerbner, 1998;McDonald, 2007): Friends or More, Past Marriages, Injuries and Birth, Family, Disagreements, Mistakes and Separation, Past Love and Life, Body Parts, Sex and Attraction, Conflicting Feelings, Financial Transactions, Breakups and Screwups, and Wedding.

A multivariate multiple regression was conducted using these topics as outcome variables and age as a covariate. Due to the use of multiple comparisons, we use the more conservative Wilks' Lambda criterion for significance test. Figure 4 shows changes in the prevalence of topics over our examined timeframe. We used a Loess smoothing (span = .5), as our interest was the general trend of change. As illustrated, Conflicting Feelings (F = 34.13, p , .001) dramatically increased over this period, with significant spikes around 2005 and 2015. Family (F = 8.58, p , .004) follows a similar trend, spiking at those same time points. Injuries and Birth (F = 7.77, p , .006) has significantly shifted over time, decreasing throughout the 1980s, then increasing with some slight dips until plummeting back to 1980s levels. Mistakes and Separation (F = 9.25, p , .003) has steadily decreased, reaching its lowest point of inclusion in 2015. Finally, Body Parts also shows a consistent decline over time (F = 6.47, p , .01).

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
paragraphs: Our investigation of 188 full scripts of top-grossing romcoms over a 40-year timespan revealed a thematic network built of two distinct themes, one dedicated to relationships and one to all other aspects of characters' lives. It also allowed for a close reading of the topics in each community and the film scenes most representative of their language. In addition to mapping the thematic content of 11,900 film scenes between 1980 and 2019, we examined dynamic changes in their use over time.

Most notably, we identified a consistent increase in the relative use of the Relationships theme in romcoms over time. This trend shifts screen time away from areas such as professional life, day jobs, and business, toward an even stronger emphasis on the dynamics of courtship and romantic relationships. Despite new plots that include characters of varied ages and life stages (e.g., It's Complicated, Second Act), resolutions other than marriage or partnership (e.g., 500 Days of Summer, Home Again), and more inclusive narratives (e.g., Crazy Rich Asians, The Big Sick), the distillation of content in favor of relationships-focused, idealistic tropes and plotlines illustrates the genre's reliance on uniform and durable messages that could result in acceptance of its social messages regarding relationships, particularly among heavy viewers (Gerbner et al., 1986), leading to the endorsement of often misleading and unrealistic romantic ideals (Hefner & Wilson, 2013;Segrin & Nabi, 2002). It seems that inclusion fits into the genre only to the extent that the same basic formula can still be applied; the couple meets and falls in love, an obstacle threatens or ends their relationship, both parties reflect on this obstacle and are ultimately united in the end. Future research should consider more detailed examinations of modern and inclusive adaptations in the genre to explore differences in relationship portrayals and audience perceptions, as these details are beyond the scope of our current results. In a similar vein and noting that our current study focuses on American culture and audiences, future content analyses might examine how relationship portrayals differ across cultures and definitions of "good" or "successful" romantic relationships.

Within the trend of increasing relationships content, the salience of specific topics has fluctuated. We focused our analyses on topics related to the construction of social expectations regarding romantic relationships, gender roles, and the interaction between the two (Gerbner, 1998;McDonald, 2007). Of the topics we examined, Injuries and Birth, Mistakes and Separation, Conflicting Feelings, and Body Parts significantly changed time, while the topics of Friends or More, Past Marriages, Past Love and Life, Sex and Attraction, Disagreements, Financial Transactions, and Wedding remained stable.

The topics that changed in prominence over time could shed light on changing cultural attitudes toward relationships. Of particular interest to our study, the Conflicting Feelings topic, the thirdmost prevalent topic in the corpus, has been steadily increasing throughout the timeframe examined here. This topic deals with many dissident experiences including liking someone as more than a friend, simultaneous gestures of thanks and apology, feeling seen but invisible, and being in a bad mood despite having fun. Films emphasizing the Conflicting Feelings topic may suggest to audiences that courtship means overcoming many hesitations, dilemmas, and challenges, demonstrating the "love conquers all" ideal found by previous researchers (Galloway, Engstrom, & Emmers-Sommer, 2015;Hefner & Wilson, 2013) and supporting Johnson and Holmes' (2009) pointed to contradictory messages within the genre. These depictions tell audiences that the experience of falling in love is inherently uncomfortable, and their persistent and increasing use may cultivate unhealthy relationships depictions in young audiences' minds (Bachen & Illouz, 1996;Johnson & Holmes, 2009). This same perspective can be seen in relationship partners who believe in "soulmates," relying on this belief rather than solving problems with communication and a "work it out" attitude (Franiuk et al., 2012).

We note that many of the topics identified align with the findings of previous content analyses. For instance, Johnson and Holmes (2009) compiled categories including "family and friend approval," "affection," "commitment," and "relationship issues," which correspond well with our topics on Family, Body Parts, Wedding, and Mistakes and Separation, respectively. Similarly, although we did not code into categories representing tropes as Hefner and Wilson (2013), we see that their expressions of "love conquers all," "idealization of other," and "soulmate" can be seen in our topics Friends or More, Sex & Attraction, and Past Love & Life, and are generally representative of content in the Relationships theme overall. We hope that future research will more deeply investigate how such tropes are associated with themes and topics.

Despite the overall consistency of the messaging, these changes reveal romcoms as an increasingly tumultuous depiction of courtship. Compared to viewers in the 1980s and 1990s, modern audiences should expect more and higher hurdles for protagonists to overcome, with more physical injuries and trouble that may involve their families or children (planned and unplanned, biological and adopted). Despite adaptations for different family structures and improved cultural representation in films like My Big Fat Greek Wedding and Crazy Rich Asians, both of which are among the top ten most popular films, romcoms ultimately and increasingly reinforce the same messaging: that no matter the obstacle, be it infidelity, deception, or extreme incompatibility, love and destiny mean ignoring, forgiving, or accepting those differences even at the expense of friends, family, or career.

Films emphasizing the Conflicting Feelings topic may suggest to audiences that courtship means overcoming many hesitations, dilemmas, and challenges, demonstrating the "love conquers all" ideal found by previous researchers (Galloway, Engstrom, & Emmers-Sommer, 2015;Hefner & Wilson, 2013) and supporting Johnson and Holmes' (2009) pointed to contradictory messages within the genre. These depictions tell audiences that the experience of falling in love is inherently uncomfortable, and their persistent and increasing use may cultivate unhealthy relationships depictions in young audiences' minds (Bachen & Illouz, 1996;Johnson & Holmes, 2009). This same perspective can be seen in relationship partners who believe in "soulmates," relying on this belief rather than solving problems with communication and a "work it out" attitude (Franiuk et al., 2012).

We note that many of the topics identified align with the findings of previous content analyses. For instance, Johnson and Holmes (2009) compiled categories including "family and friend approval," "affection," "commitment," and "relationship issues," which correspond well with our topics on Family, Body Parts, Wedding, and Mistakes and Separation, respectively. Similarly, although we did not code into categories representing tropes as Hefner and Wilson (2013), we see that their expressions of "love conquers all," "idealization of other," and "soulmate" can be seen in our topics Friends or More, Sex & Attraction, and Past Love & Life, and are generally representative of content in the Relationships theme overall. We hope that future research will more deeply investigate how such tropes are associated with themes and topics.

Despite the overall consistency of the messaging, these changes reveal romcoms as an increasingly tumultuous depiction of courtship. Compared to viewers in the 1980s and 1990s, modern audiences should expect more and higher hurdles for protagonists to overcome, with more physical injuries and trouble that may involve their families or children (planned and unplanned, biological and adopted). Despite adaptations for different family structures and improved cultural representation in films like My Big Fat Greek Wedding and Crazy Rich Asians, both of which are among the top ten most popular films, romcoms ultimately and increasingly reinforce the same messaging: that no matter the obstacle, be it infidelity, deception, or extreme incompatibility, love and destiny mean ignoring, forgiving, or accepting those differences even at the expense of friends, family, or career.
sections.preview_head: Our investigation of 188 full scripts of top-grossing romcoms over a 40-year timespan revealed a thematic network built of two distinct themes, one dedicated to relationships and one to all other aspects of characters' lives. It also allowed for a close reading of the topics in each community and the film scenes most representative of their language. In addition to mapping the thematic content of 11,900 film scenes between 1980 and 2019, we examined dynamic changes in their use over time.

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
paragraphs: Several caveats should be noted. First, this work examines only the text of films, the dialogue, and thus does not capture the full viewer experience. As is the case with any expert-leaden content analysis, we do not claim that the way we experience the text would be equivalent to the way audiences would. For one, unobserved variables, such as soundtrack, lighting, costumes, and directors' notes contribute to films' messages and content, can enhance or contradict linguistic messages, and cannot be captured via text analysis.

Once again, there is a tension between breadth and depth of corpus analysis, or any big data study (see Shah et al., 2015). But we believe that some phenomena can only be observed from a bird'seye view, as argued by scholars of digital humanities, distant reading (Moretti, 2013) and macroanalysis (Jockers, 2013). Likewise, we see our work as complementing, and not replacing, close reading and audience reception studies. The decision to focus on the text alone was made to allow access to a vast amount of data in the form of the full scripts of almost 200 films. Notably, the goal of the ANTMN methodology is to eliminate and reduce bias as much as possible (Walter & Ophir, 2019). By utilizing this computational method, we were able to examine a wider range and number of films than previous analyses of the genre. Still, we acknowledge that there were human interventions, including the decision to analyze films on the scene level (commonly considered the base level of a film; Murtagh et al., 2009) and in labeling the topics and themes based on qualitative expert readings (standard practice for ANTMN and topic modeling; Baden, 2018;Maier et al., 2018;Walter & Ophir, 2019).

In addition, as explained in the method section, some scripts included directors' notes while others did not, and we prioritized standardization across the corpus and removed them all. Despite this important limitation, the dialogue alone could represent important trends in content and messaging across the genre. Future studies of the genre could shed more light on the intersection of dialogue and other aspects to better capture these details. Similarly, future research might more deeply explore the differences between and changes across subgenres, for instance, comparing teen and adult romcoms, screwball comedies, and neoliberal romcoms.

Notably, our findings reflect an analysis of contemporary films released after 1980; from a genre that has roots dating back to the 1920s. We chose to analyze the top-grossing films of all time, resulting in a cut-off date of 1980 (omitting only two of 200 from 1979). Difficulty in obtaining these older scripts aside, computational text analysis benefits from corpora's linguistic coherency. If the language in films from the 1920s was substantially different from today's scripts (which could be expected), the model is likely to separate them from contemporary scripts, making it harder to identify broader themes. Our findings, however, should be interpreted as representing contemporary romcoms, and not the historical roots of the genre.

Further, empirical studies are needed to examine the actual effects of the topics and themes identified here. We hope that our findings will lay the groundwork for such experimentation. In addition, while we have identified trends in content over time, more detailed analysis is needed to investigate the potential motivations for the content shifts revealed here, such as financial success, critical acclaim, or positive audience ratings. This may include detailed examination of the tropes contained in the genre, as it would be relevant to the discussion of audience effects to determine whether trope-laden content is increasing within the Relationships theme. Indeed, we have conducted some preliminary coding of such content that leads us to believe this is the case. Further examination may reveal whether these tropes are associated with specific topics.

We also acknowledge that the use of unofficial sources for scripts may have resulted in occasional inaccuracies, due to actors not speaking their lines verbatim or transcription errors. However, based on our close examination of all scripts we believe that such inaccuracies were minimal and should not have impacted the validity of a textual model.

sections.paper_id: Moore and Ophir - 2022 - Big data, actually Examining systematic messaging in 188 romantic comedies using unsupervised machi
paragraphs: Despite these caveats, the current study presents a novel approach for the systematic analysis of a big textual data derived from entertainment media. We combined computational methods with close, expert-based qualitative reading of texts to map the linguistic foundations of the romcom, to examine the meaning of the most prominent topics, and to statistically examine trends over time. We demonstrate that, in contrast to arguments by researchers and film theorists, romcoms are consistently and increasingly focusing on the protagonist couple's relationship and dedicating less screen time to developing their lives independent from romance.

As prior research pointed to tendency of depiction of relationships in the genre to be unrealistic, misleading, and toxic, this finding could potentially induce social concern. We encourage future empirical research on the direct effects of this shift on audiences, potential treatments that could counteract those effects, and potential motivators of these trends within the film industry.
